stem: A guy wire is attached to the top of a 120-foot radio antenna and anchored to the ground. The wire makes a 53-degree angle with the ground. Given that sin 53° ≈ 4/5, cos 53° ≈ 3/5, and tan 53° ≈ 4/3, what is the length of the guy wire, in feet?
explanation: The antenna height (120 feet) is the side opposite the 53° angle, and the guy wire is the hypotenuse. Using sine: sin 53° = opposite/hypotenuse = 120/wire. Solving for the wire: wire = 120 ÷ sin 53° = 120 ÷ (4/5) = 120 × (5/4) = 150 feet.

distractorRationale.A: Divided by tan instead of sin, finding the horizontal distance to the anchor instead of the wire length: 120 ÷ tan 53° = 120 ÷ (4/3) = 120 × (3/4) = 90.
distractorRationale.B: Multiplied by sin instead of dividing, inverting the relationship between opposite and hypotenuse: 120 × sin 53° = 120 × (4/5) = 96.
distractorRationale.C: Divided by cos instead of sin, confusing adjacent and opposite in the ratio: 120 ÷ cos 53° = 120 ÷ (3/5) = 120 × (5/3) = 200.

imageDescription: A right triangle with the vertical leg labeled as a 120 ft radio antenna. The hypotenuse represents the guy wire, labeled with a question mark, running from the top of the antenna down to an anchor point on the ground. The angle between the wire and the horizontal ground is labeled 53°.
